title: Boundary-Robust Transmission Asymmetry as a Topological Signature in Open Floquet Lattices
authors: Ren Zhang, Xiao-Yu Ouyang, Xu-Dong Dai, Xi Dai
abstract: We identify a boundary-robust topological signature of open Floquet lattices: although nonadiabatic boundaries strongly reshape the transmission lineshape, the integrated left--right transmission asymmetry saturates to a plateau set by the bulk Floquet winding number. Its origin is a deep-bulk branch-population principle: in the long-sample limit, each propagating Floquet--Bloch branch is generically populated with unit weight, since true Floquet bound states are nongeneric. The robust observable is therefore the cumulative transmission imbalance rather than the boundary-sensitive transmission profile. We propose direct detection by cold-atom transmission spectroscopy. For electronic transport, the same asymmetry admits contact-model-dependent electrical readouts: a coherent Floquet--Landauer--Büttiker interpretation predicts a near-\(2ef\) response in weak SAW devices, whereas a blocking-factor post-processing yields a qualitatively different signal.
summary: This paper identifies a boundary-robust topological signature in open Floquet lattices: the integrated transmission asymmetry. While realistic, non-adiabatic boundaries distort the detailed transmission spectrum, the cumulative asymmetry remains locked to the bulk winding number. This robustness arises because propagating Floquet-Bloch branches are generically fully populated. The authors propose detecting this via cold-atom spectroscopy and electronic transport in SAW-driven devices.
main_problem: Determining whether bulk Floquet topology can be extracted from transmission observables in open systems, given that non-adiabatic boundaries distort pointwise transmission profiles.
main_result: The integrated left-right transmission asymmetry is a robust topological signature that saturates to a plateau determined by the bulk Floquet winding number, due to the generic openness of propagating branches.
method: The authors use a theoretical framework based on the deep-bulk branch-population principle and asymptotic analysis, supplemented by numerical Monte Carlo sampling and proposed experimental setups in cold atoms and SAW devices.
model_or_system: Open Floquet lattices (periodically driven lattices) coupled to asymptotic leads, including models for cold-atom transmission spectroscopy and electronic transport in SAW-driven piezoelectric nanowires.
key_observables: Integrated left-right transmission asymmetry, smoothed long-sample transmittance, and zero-bias electrical current.
important_parameters: Bulk Floquet winding number, quasienergy, lattice length (long-sample limit), boundary ramp length, and driving frequency.
main_assumptions: The results rely on the long-sample limit (L approaching infinity) and the assumption of a regular quasienergy window with non-zero group velocities.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the convergence of smoothed transmission spectra; Figure 2 demonstrates the robustness of integrated asymmetry against boundary changes; Figure 3 and 4 illustrate the proposed cold-atom and SAW experimental setups; Figure 5 and 6 compare electrical transport predictions under different contact models.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of boundary-induced distortion, develops the deep-bulk branch-population principle to prove robustness, provides a mathematical proof of generic openness, and concludes with proposed experimental implementations in cold atoms and electronic transport.
why_interesting: It provides a new, robust way to detect topological invariants in driven, open systems where traditional pointwise signatures are destroyed by boundary effects, which is highly relevant for experimentalists working with Floquet engineering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Construction of the smoothed transmission spectrum. Raw transmission curves for sample lengths L = 4, 8, 16 exhibit dense Fabry–P´erot oscillations, whereas the energy-window smoothed curves rapidly converge. In the L →∞limit, the smoothed transmission becomes a well- defined local coarse-grained observable that effectively filters out finite-size interference.

FIG. 2. Boundary robustness of the integrated spec- tral asymmetry. For an open Floquet lattice with V (x, t) = ξ(x)Vbulk(x, t) and Vbulk(x, t) = 0.8ER cos(2πx−ωt)+0.2ER, the boundary envelope ξ(x) implements a linear ramp from 0 to 1 over a length Lramp. While varying Lramp strongly distorts the smoothed transmission spectra ¯T L/R(E), the in- tegrated asymmetry A(E0) remains invariant and saturates to a stable plateau. This plateau height equals the rigid spec- tral shift dictated by the bulk winding number in the adia- batic limit, demonstrating that the topological signature is immune to microscopic boundary details.

FIG. 3. Schematic of the cold-atom Bragg-scattering setup. Two counter-propagating laser beams with a con- trolled frequency detuning, implemented via an acousto-optic modulator (AOM), generate a moving optical lattice. A Bose– Einstein condensate (BEC) serves as the scattering medium, confined by a quasi-1D red-detuned optical waveguide to re- alize a strictly one-dimensional scattering geometry.

FIG. 5. Zero-bias current versus SAW drive strength and chemical potential within the coherent-scattering prescription. Numerical results use hard-wall boundary conditions—a conservative estimate for which realistic soft boundaries should yield sharper quantization. A near-2ef re- sponse emerges over a broad parameter regime within this prescription, illustrating a possible electrical readout of the boundary-robust integrated transmission asymmetry.

FIG. 4. Schematic of the SAW-driven zero-bias trans- port device. An interdigitated transducer (IDT) on a piezo- electric substrate (e.g., LiNbO3) launches a unidirectional sur- face acoustic wave, terminated by an absorber to suppress re- flections. The traveling strain field induces a moving potential in a proximal nanowire (e.g., InSb). By tuning the chemical potential via a back-gate and maintaining zero source–drain bias, the resulting SAW-driven transport current is measured, providing a possible electrical readout of the integrated spec- tral asymmetry within the coherent-scattering interpretation.

FIG. 6. Experimentally distinguishable transport predictions. Left: zero-bias current from the coher- ent prescription [Eq. (12)], showing a weakly temperature- dependent near-2ef plateau. Right: zero-bias current from the blocking-factor prescription [Eq. (14)], obtained from the same sideband-resolved transmission data [Eq. (13)]. The plateau is suppressed and the temperature dependence is much stronger. The driving amplitude is fixed at V = 0.8ER, with all other parameters the same as in Fig. 5.
